Li, Meiyu
Liu, Yize
Zhao, Tengda
Zhang, Ruoyao

coredata.dc:description: Trace Co2+, when present in large quantities, is harmful to the environment and therefore cannot be ignored. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) is a standard method used to detect metal ions, however, detecting trace Co2+ under high saline conditions can be challenging. Similarly, existing Co2+ treatment methods are prone to secondary pollution and have high energy consumption. Therefore, it is necessary to find an efficient and non-polluting method for Co2+ detection and treatment. This study successfully synthesized nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) based on natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES) using a one-step solvothermal method. The prepared N-CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence and high salt tolerance. The simultaneous detection and treatment of trace Co2+ in water under high salinity conditions were achieved for the first time. The response of the N-CQDs to Co2+ under saline condition was linear in the range of 5–250 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2269 μM. Feasibility of practical application was assessed by quantitative detection of Co2+ in real water samples. Furthermore, the N-CQDs can treat Co2+, and the removal rate was 99.98%.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trace Co2+, when present in large quantities, is harmful to the environment and therefore cannot be ignored. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) is a standard method used to detect metal ions, however, detecting trace Co2+ under high saline conditions can be challenging. Similarly, existing Co2+ treatment methods are prone to secondary pollution and have high energy consumption. Therefore, it is necessary to find an efficient and non-polluting method for Co2+ detection and treatment. This study successfully synthesized nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) based on natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES) using a one-step solvothermal method. The prepared N-CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence and high salt tolerance. The simultaneous detection and treatment of trace Co2+ in water under high salinity conditions were achieved for the first time. The response of the N-CQDs to Co2+ under saline condition was linear in the range of 5–250 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2269 μM. Feasibility of practical application was assessed by quantitative detection of Co2+ in real water samples. Furthermore, the N-CQDs can treat Co2+, and the removal rate was 99.98%.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Simultaneous detection and treatment of trace Co2+

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt, a vital trace element, promotes enzyme synthesis and enhances enzymatic activity in organisms (Ashton, 1970). Furthermore, metallic cobalt is the primary component of vitamin B-12 and thiamine (Thevis et al., 2020). According to WHO guidelines, the average dietary intake of cobalt by humans daily ranges between 5 and 40 μg (Kim et al., 2006). However, excessive cobalt consumption can lead to increased erythropoiesis and blood glucose, myocardial injury, and even death. In recent years, cobalt has become a common dopant and catalyst in the production of new materials and renewable energy, thereby contributing to sustainable development technologies (Qiang et al., 2008). However, it is also widely used in electroplating and dye industries, mining, and metal alloys, and improper handling can cause severe environmental pollution (Zhang et al., 2017). Traditional methods for detecting Co2+ mainly include atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AAS) (Simmons and W, 1973), spectrophotometry (Ray et al., 1976), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Douglas and D. J, 1986). With AAS, the detection of single Co2+ in a complex environment is often inaccurate due to the low concentration of Co2+. In addition, different light sources need to be replaced when testing different metal ions. Detection of heavy metal ions by spectrophotometry requires a specific chelator to generate dark-colored chelates. ICP-MS is a standard method for the determination of trace Co2+. However, industrial wastewater containing cobalt often contains a considerable amount of salt (e.g., electroplating and dye industries) (Lefebvre and Moletta, 2006). Detection of trace Co2+ under highly saline conditions is difficult using ICP-MS. Traditional Co2+ treatment techniques mainly include chemical precipitation (Mauchauffee and Meux, 2007), adsorption (Kara et al., 2003), membrane filtration (Zhou et al., 1994), and electrochemical processes (Dermentzis et al., 2010). Although the chemical precipitation is easy to conduct, reducing the concentration of metal ions below the ppm-level is difficult. The adsorption method has poor selectivity and low recovery (Kang et al., 2016). Any Co2+ released is likely to result in secondary pollution in the membrane regeneration and adsorption. The electrochemical process is not suitable for large-scale applications owing to its high energy consumption and cost. In particular, the co-existence of inorganic salts dramatically reduces the efficiency of Co2+ detection and removal by these technologies. Furthermore, the current Co2+ detection and treatment process consists of two parts, significantly complicating the removal of contaminant Co2+. Therefore, it is urgently necessary to find a method with high selectivity, strong sensitivity, rapid response time, good salt tolerance, for simultaneous detection and treatment of Co2+.

Novel carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received considerable attention in the past decade. Among them, CQDs have been widely used in photocatalysis (Shaohai et al., 2018) (Liu et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2016), electrochemistry (Xu et al., 2014), biological imaging (Molaei, 2019), fluorescence sensing (Yang et al., 2021) and various other research fields owing to their low cost, environmental friendliness, and high fluorescence among other characteristics. Furthermore, CQDs and other zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials have been used as fluorescent probes for sensitive detection of heavy metal ions (Jayan et al., 2021; Placido et al., 2019b; Salahinejad et al., 2021; Singh et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2021). In 2017, Y. L. Liu et al. used sodium citrate and polyacrylamide as precursors to prepare carbon quantum dots successfully with bright blue fluorescence by a one-step hydrothermal method, which could recognize Pb2+ and establish the off-on system by using pyrophosphate (Liu et al., 2017). Jiang et al. (2018) prepared CQDs to detect Fe3+, wherein the deep eutectic solvents (DES) act as a solvent (Jiang et al., 2018). With the deepening of the research, common CQDs have some shortcomings, such as low fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY), fewer active sites, and poor selectivity. In one study by a British team, CQDs were obtained by chemical depolymerization and solution purification of microalgae biochar, which had good photobleaching resistance but poor selectivity (Placido et al., 2019a). CQDs could realize the detection of four heavy metal ions (Cu2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+). In addition, they proposed to solve the problem by modifying CQDs with nitrogen and phosphorus groups. The same year, Zeinab et al. (2019) synthesized highly fluorescent N-doped carbon dots for detecting Au3+ ions sensitively and selectively (Zeinab et al., 2019).

Since then, more researchers have proposed that the nitrogen-containing groups can suppress non-radiative electron-hole pair recombination, leading to high FLQY of CQDs. Therefore, N-CQDs show excellent photoelectric properties, and have considerable potential in detecting cobalt-containing wastewater. For instance, in some previous studies (Bano et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021), the prepared N-CQDs with different precursors could function as a probe for Co2+ detection. However, thus far, no N-CQDs have achieved the dual objectives of Co2+ detection and treatment.

Natural deep eutectic solvents (NADESs) are a kind of recently-emerged green solvents (Paiva et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2018). The designable character of NADESs would provide sufficient reactive sites by optimizing the kinds and molar ratio of natural precursors. Meanwhile, the functionalized structure would facilitate the exploration of the CQDs formation mechanism. In this study, we synthesized N-CQDs based on NADESs by a one-step solvothermal method, which could simultaneously detect and treat Co2+ under saline conditions. The structural characteristics of the as-synthesized N-CQDs were first evaluated, and the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs are discussed in details. Furthermore, the effects of high salinity and other common interfering substances on the N-CQDs detection and treatment system for Co2+ were investigated. In comparison with previous studies, this study achieved simultaneous detection and treatment of trace Co2+ under high salinity conditions using N-CQDs. On application of the method to the actual wastewater sample, the removal rate of Co2+ in wastewater was found to be 99.98%. Additionally, the luminescence mechanism of N-CQDs and the relevant reaction mechanism of N-CQDs and Co2+ were revealed by spectrum detection.

1H-Benzotriazole (BTA), choline chloride, anhydrous ethanol, cobalt sulfate, sodium sulfate, sodium chloride, cobalt 2-ethylcaproate, sodium 2-ethylhexanoate, 2-ethylhexanoic acid, and barium chloride were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cobalt-containing wastewater was obtained from Shanghai Huayi Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Magnesium(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Mg (NO3)2·6H2O), cuprum(II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu (NO3)2·3H2O), chromic(III) nitrate nonahydrate (Cr (NO3)3·9H2O), aluminum(III) nitrate nonahydrate (Al (NO3)3·9H2O), lead(II) nitrate (Pb (NO3)2), ferric(III) nitrate nonahydrate (Fe (NO3)3·9H2O), were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as received without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using an F20 TEM (JEM-2100, Hitachi, Japan), with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Energy disperse spectroscopy (EDS) and mapping images were collected using an energy dispersive spectrometer (Aztec Xmax 50, Oxford). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al K Alpha (hv = 1340 eV) in standard lens mode (ThermoFischer, Waltham, USA). Zeta potential measurements were carried out on a malvern nano-ZS90 (Malvern, UK). An inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS, iCAP Q, Thermo, Waltham, USA) was used to measure element concentrations. Nuclear magnetic spectra (1H NMR, 13C NMR) were recorded using a nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker, Avance 500 MHz, Germany) using D2O as the solvent. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained via the KBr pellet method using a Bruker Tensor 27 (Germany) spectrophotometer with wavenumbers from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The in situ infrared spectrometer system (Mettler Toledo, React IR 15, Switzerland) was used to detect chemical reactions over the entire course of the experiment to analyze the mechanism and process of the reaction. Ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-6100S UV–vis spectrophotometer (Metash instrument Co., Ltd., China) with a 1.0-cm quartz cuvette. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a 150 W lamp as the excitation source. Time-resolved luminescence decays and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) were acquired using an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CQDs based on NADESs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, NADESs based on BTA and choline chloride were prepared following previously established methods (Hayyan et al., 2014). BTA and choline chloride were added into a 250 mL flask at molar ratios of 2.5:1. The mixtures were heated with stirring until the homogeneous liquid phase appeared. The product was dried under vacuum at 90 °C for 4 h, and the product was obtained. The N-CQDs were synthesized using a solvothermal method with minor modifications (You et al., 2013). Briefly, 1 g of NADESs and 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol were added to 50 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 210 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the product was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the obtained supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μM filter (Khan et al., 2020).

In order to evaluate the influence of temperature on the fluorescence stability, 10 mL N-CQD solution was added to 10 mL centrifuge tubes and maintained at 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C for 1 h respectively. The solution was transferred to the cuvette quickly and analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The effect of storage time was detected after the N-CQDs solution was kept at 25 °C for 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 days later.

A solution with varying pH (1–14) was prepared using sodium hydroxide and 37% concentrated hydrochloric acid following a previously established protocol (Chao et al., 2020). To determine the influence of pH on the fluorescence stability, 4 mL N-CQDs and 1 mL prepared pH solution from 1 to 14 were added to 10 mL centrifuge tubes, respectively. Subsequently, the mixtures were mixed ultrasonically. The fluorescence spectra were detected by sequence at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm.

The selectivity of N-CQDs for heavy metal ions was explored by comparing the effects of N-CQDs with seven different metal ions. Initially, solutions (500 μM) of seven metal ions (Al3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Co2+) were prepared for further use. Subsequently, N-CQDs were mixed with the above solutions in a 1:1 volume ratio and added into centrifuge tubes. Later, all centrifuge tubes were shaken thoroughly for 10 s and transferred to a spectrophotometer quartz cuvette for fluorescence spectra recording after 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and treatment performance under saline conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate N-CQDs' Co2+ detection and treatment performance under saline conditions, we selected two typical salts, sodium sulfate and sodium chloride. Subsequently, a series of sodium chloride and sodium sulfate solutions with different concentrations were prepared for later use. In addition, we also prepared a series of Co2+ solutions with a gradient of concentration (0.0001–1000 μM). A mixture of N-CQDs and Co2+ with volume ratio 1:1 was used as the control group, the volume ratio of N-CQDs, salt solution, and cobalt ion solution was 2:1:1 to ensure that the concentration of Co2+ after mixing was same as the control group. Finally, the mixed solution was placed in a cuvette for the fluorescence test, and the fluorescence intensity changes in the presence and absence of salt solution were compared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Co2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt-containing wastewater diluted 15 times was mixed with N-CQDs at a 1:1 volume ratio to observe the reaction. Once the reaction was complete, it was immediately transferred to a cuvette for a fluorescence test and the fluorescence quenching rate was calculated.

Cobalt-containing wastewater from Shanghai Huayi Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. mainly contains cobalt (II) sulfate hydrate (CoSO4), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), 2-ethylhexanoic Acid (C8H16O2), sodium 2-ethylhexanoate (C8H15O2Na), cobalt (II) 2-ethylhexanoate (C16H30O4Co). Therefore, we introduced C8H16O2 and C8H15O2Na into the experimental environment to further verify the anti-interference of N-CQDs. Furthermore, we also measured the concentration of Co2+ using ICP-MS and judged the type of Co2+ in the wastewater according to the fluorescence quenching rate. Finally, the fluorescence intensity changes of different concentrations of Co2+ on N-CQDs were observed through UV - Camera Obscura. The fluorescence quenching rate and the concentration of Co2+ were linear and nonlinear fitting, which could realize the effective detection of Co2+ in a specific concentration range. Additionally, the effective treatment of Co2+ was be achieved by the reaction of N-CQDs and cobalt-containing wastewater.

N-CQDs were synthesized via the one-step solvothermal method. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image is shown in Fig. 1(a). The obtained N-CQDs are spherical and distributed evenly. The particle size distribution (inserted in Fig. 1(a)) shows that the N-CQDs were 2–8 nm in size with an average diameter of 4.76 nm. The contrast examples under different reaction conditions are shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information).

We also tested the zeta potential of N-CQDs. In our experimental design, the testing value was −19.9 mV, proving a specific theoretical basis for the reaction between the prepared N-CQDs and positively charged metal ions. Therefore, it was speculated that there may be many ionizable functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs. The data diagram is shown in Fig. S2.

In order to verify that nitrogen atoms were doped successfully, EDS and mapping were used to analyze the types of elements and the atomic proportion of N-CQDs (Fig. 1(b)). The concentration of each element and atomic percentage are shown in Table S1. Carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and chlorine atoms accounted for 55.92%, 15.38%, 26.76% and 1.95%, respectively. The surface scanning results show that the elements were distributed evenly, and nitrogen atoms were doped successfully with high content to the surface of N-CQDs (Fig. (c–f)).

Moreover, XPS measurements were carried out to explore the chemical bonding between the elements. Fig. 2(a) shows the wide full scan XPS spectrum with four distinct peaks at 290.9 eV, 408.9 eV, 536.9 eV, and 201.9 eV (202.9 eV), which corresponds to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Cl2p3, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2(b) shows three peaks with binding energies around 283.7 eV, 284.2 eV, and 285.5 eV, which are assigned to CC/CC, CN, CO/COH bonds (Chen et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2017b). The N1s spectrum in Fig. 2(c) is deconvoluted into four peaks with binding energies at 399.9 eV, 401.4 eV, 402.6 eV, and 406.5 eV attributed to CNC, CNH, NH4 + and NO3 − respectively. Fig. 2(d) shows O1s spectrum of bonds CO (532.0 eV) and 7 COC/COH (532.7 eV) (Lashkor et al., 2014). Moreover, finally, the peaks located at 197.4 eV, 198.7 eV and 199.2 eV for N-CQDs are assigned to the binding energies of Cl 2P, Cl 2P3 and CCl, respectively (Fig. 2(e)). To sum up, there is an army of hydrophilic groups on the surfaces of the N-CQDs, such as OH, NH, COC/COH, and CO groups, resulting in the excellent solubility and stability of the N-CQDs in water. Results of XPS show the existence of amide groups and confirm the doping of nitrogen atoms in the prepared N-CQDs.

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to determine the surface functional groups of N-CQDs. BTA and choline chloride form hydrogen bonds in the formation of NADESs accompanied by weakening of NH and OH bond strength in 3249.96 cm−1 (Fig. 3(a)) and 3275.75 cm−1 (Fig. 3(b)), respectively. As a result, the stretching vibrations of the two bonds are reduced. On the contrary, the stretching vibration of the CH bond on the phenyl at 2993.43 cm−1 is more prominent (Fig. 3(c)). In addition, other chemical bonds have not changed substantially, indicating that hydrogen bonds only form NADESs and no chemical reaction occurs in this process. However, high temperature leads to the breaking of hydrogen bonds in NADESs during the formation of N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the peak at 3369.54 cm−1 corresponds to the amide group's NH stretching vibration (Zhao et al., 2019). Besides, the double peaks at 2985.72 and 2898.93 cm−1 are attributed to CH stretching vibration, most likely from Carboxyl (COOH) and carbonyl (COOR) group. The weak peak at 1620.12 cm−1 is attributed to the CC stretching vibration of the olefin.

Moreover, the double absorption peaks at 1454.28 and 1394.49 cm−1 with a slight red shift relative to Fig. 3(a) are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC on phenyl, a weak peak at 883.37 cm−1 corresponding to CH bending vibration on phenyl further indicates the presence of polyaromatic structures of the N-CQDs (Wang et al., 2017a). The sharp and weak doublet at 1267.20 and 1211.26 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibration CH, most likely from the phenylamino group with polarity and alkalinity. The sharp peaks at 1055.03 cm−1 and 746.43 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of CN and the out-of-plane bending vibration of OH. The FT-IR results showed that the N-CQDs possessed a mass of conjugated benzene structure and nitrogen groups, which significantly contributed to the high FLQY of the N-CQDs. Overall, the XPS analyses are in accordance with the FT-IR results. It also provides a theoretical basis for verifying zeta potential results (Chen et al., 2021).

1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of the N-CQDs served as an assistant to characterize the types of carbon and hydrogen in the N-CQDs. In Fig. 3(e), It can be observed that the peak at 1.06, 3.52, and 4.66 ppm were attributable to R–OH, Ar–OH, and –CHO of N-CQDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(f), the 13C NMR spectra are mainly divided into four peak groups, wherein the region ① represented by the peak at 16.77 ppm is attributed to –CH2–. The peak group ② near 57.23 ppm is assigned to CO, and CN. Peak groups ③ and ④ are centered on 114.62, and 126.33 ppm represent carbon atomic absorption of the benzene, CN, and CC, respectively. The types of hydrogen and carbon obtained from this method are consistent with the above results.

As shown in Fig. 4(a), distinct absorption peaks are focused on 257 and 313 nm with approximate height and symmetric shape in the UV–Vis spectrum of N-CQDs (the black line), which are assigned to the π–π* transition of the conjugated phenyl band and the n–π* transition of the CO band. Conventionally, the former originates from sp2 carbon in the core (Ding et al., 2016). The excitation spectrum extends from 300 nm to 360 nm. It is composed of only a strong band with a maximum at 327 nm, which is close to the absorption band in the long-wavelength region and likely originates from n–π* transitions in molecular fluorophores presented on the N-CQDs (the blue line) (Hu et al., 2013; Reckmeier et al., 2015). Under 325 nm excitation, the N-CQDs emit blue light (as shown in the insert diagram) with a maximum of 375 nm (the red line). Fig. 4(b) is the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths from 314 nm to 360 nm, the maximum emission changing beyond this range. Essentially, the excitation-dependent PL behavior is related to surface states affecting the band gap of obtained N-CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 4(c), the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs is 2.8 ns. Additionally, the FLQY of the N-CQDs was determined to be 25% under the optimal excitation at 340 nm in Fig. S3.

In order to investigate the effect of temperature on fluorescence intensity, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was measured at 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C, respectively. Fig. 4(d) illustrates the fluorescence intensity for N-CQDs derived at different temperatures. It is seen that the change of temperature doesn't result in an obvious variation in the fluorescence intensity except for a slight reduction at 60 °C. Overall, the fluorescence intensity remains stable, indicating that the N-CQDs have high-intensity fluorescence performance and a wide range of temperature applications.

Exploring the change of fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs under the number of days 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 (Fig. 4(e)), the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly and irregularly overall, which may be related to the agglomeration of N elements in the N-CQDs.

To explore the effect of pH on N-CQDs, the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs in various pH values from 1 to 14 are listed in Fig. 4(f). As can be seen, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs is lower under strongly basic conditions (pH = 14), but remains unchanged under other conditions (pH = 1–13). In general, the prepared N-CQDs are only suitable for pH conditions other than strongly basic conditions. It indicates that N-CQDs have good pH stability and could provide favorable usage without additional pH adjustment for subsequent detection.

Fig. 5(a) shows the emission spectra of the obtained N-CQDs in different concentrations of Co2+ solution. Upon increasing the concentration of Co2+, the PL intensity of the N-CQDs shows a gradual reduction. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the fluorescence quenching rate ((F0 F)/F0) of N-CQDs and concentration of Co2+ are fitted within the concentration range of 0.0001–500 μM. It conforms to the nonlinear relation, defined by the equation is (F0 F)/F0 = 133.112–128.538/(1 + (x/254.9975)^1.0519), with correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9861. Fig. 5(c) shows the response of the N-CQDs toward Co2+ at various concentrations from 5 to 250 μM. Excellent linearities ((F0 F)/F0 = 0.2713× + 6.72137) with correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9867 were obtained, for which LOD of 1.3426 μM was derived (LOD = 3δ/S), δ is the standard deviation of the blank samples (δ = 0.12142). S denotes the slope of this equation ((F0 F)/F0 = 0.2713× + 6.72137, S = 0.2713). This value is below emission standard 16.9684 μM (1.0 mg/L). Furthermore, the insert of Fig. 5(c) shows the blue fluorescent of N-CQDs weakened progressively when Co2+ was added under the UV light at 302 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Co2+ under saline condition
ce:section.ce:para: This study investigated the effects of Na2SO4 and NaCl, which are always present in industrial wastewater, on detection of Co2+ by N-CQDs. First, the effect of Na2SO4 alone on the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs was verified. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the fluorescent quenching degree (F/F0) does not change at low Na2SO4 concentrations (0 to 100 mM). However, in the high concentration range (250 to 2000 mM), the value of F/F0 decreases significantly with the increase of Na2SO4 concentration. In the insert of Fig. 6(a), solid Na2SO4 precipitated out of the mixed solution of N-CQDs and Na2SO4. Furthermore, to explore whether the presence of Na2SO4 has an impact on the detection of Co2+ by N-CQDs, we conducted fluorescence spectrum analysis for the coexistence system of the three elements.

It can be seen from Fig. 6(b) that the fluorescence quenching rate of Co2+ on N-CQDs is 34.9% without interference. The quenching phenomenon was obvious under UV light of 302 nm, with the fluorescence becoming dark (left). However, under the existence of Na2SO4, the fluorescence quenching rate decreased to 5.4%, and the fluorescence became bright (right). It is concluded that Na2SO4 has a great influence on the detection of the Co2+ system by N-CQDs.

It was verified by the same method that the effect of NaCl on the detection of Co2+ by N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the fluorescence quenching rates of Co2+ on N-CQDs were 43.2% and 34.9% in the absence and presence of NaCl, respectively. The corresponding image under UV light of 302 nm has not changed much. Furthermore, fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs with different Co2+ concentrations were plotted under certain NaCl concentrations (Fig. 6(d)). Fluorescence quenching rates of N-CQDs and Co2+ concentration can still be fitted linearly and nonlinearly. From Fig. 6(e), (F0 F)/F0 of N-CQDs and concentration of Co2+ are fitted within the concentration range of 0.5 to 400 μM under certain NaCl concentrations. It conforms to the nonlinear relation, defined by the equation (F0 F)/F0 = 96.487–93.5398/(1 + (x/120.290)^1.435), with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9942. Besides, Fig. 6(f) shows the response of the N-CQDs toward Co2+ at various concentrations from 5 to 250 μM. Excellent linearities ((F0 F)/F0 = 0.2969x + 7.19682) with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9312 were obtained, for which LOD of 1.2269 μM was derived. This value is below emission standard 16.9684 μM (1.0 mg/L). In summary, Na2SO4 affects the detection of the Co2+ system by N-CQDs, but NaCl does not affect the detection of the Co2+ system by N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Co2+ under other interferences
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of C8H15O2Na and C8H16O2 on the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs were also investigated. In Fig. 7(a), different concentrations of C8H15O2Na solution have almost no effect on the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs in the range of 0 to 500,000 μM. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the fluorescence quenching rates of Co2+ for N-CQDs are 34.9% and 33.9% in the absence and presence of C8H15O2Na respectively, which are the same. The images had not changed under UV light of 302 nm.

Hence, fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs with different Co2+ concentrations were plotted under certain C8H15O2Na concentrations (Fig. 7(c)). Fluorescence quenching rates of N-CQDs and Co2+ concentration can still be fitted linearly and nonlinearly. In Fig. 7(d), (F0 F)/F0 of N-CQDs and concentration of Co2+ are fitted within the concentration range of 0.1 to 400 μM under certain C8H15O2Na concentrations. It conforms to the nonlinear relation, defined by the equation (F0 F)/F0 = 119.3074–114.9134/(1 + (x/189.0272)^1.108)，with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9984. Besides, the insert of Fig. 7(d) shows the response of the N-CQDs toward Co2+ at various concentrations from 1 to 250 μM. Excellent linearities ((F0 F)/F0 = 0.2697× + 8.1514) with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9804 were obtained, for which LOD of 1.3506 μM was derived. This value is below emission standard 16.9684 μM (1.0 mg/L) as well.

Then, it can be seen from Fig. 7(e) that the fluorescence spectrum changed after N-CQDs and C8H16O2 were mixed at different volume ratios. N-CQDs' fluorescence intensity did not change significantly when the ratio of N-CQDs and C8H16O2 was in the range of 1:0.01 to 1:0.1. In the range of 1:0.3 to 1:1, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs increased sharply.

In addition, C8H16O2 had no fluorescence from Fig. 7(f). The fluorescence quenching ability of Co2+ to N-CQDs changed hardly when the ratio of N-CQDs to C8H16O2 was 1:1. Therefore, C8H15O2Na and C8H16O2 have no effect on the detection of the Co2+ system by N-CQDs.

Moreover, this work verified the selectivity and specificity of N-CQDs in detecting Co2+. As shown in Fig. 8 , only N-CQDs produced obvious precipitation after mixing with cobalt-containing wastewater among various CQDs. Subsequently, it can be seen from Fig. S4 that Co2+ has the most obvious fluorescence quenching effect on N-CQDs among various metal ions. Therefore, N-CQDs can realize the specific identification and detection of Co2+ in wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of interaction between N-CQDs and Co2+
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching process of the N-CQDs with Co2+ was investigated with in situ IR spectroscopy by contrast (Fig. 9a). It could observe obviously that there are two reduced platforms from 1350 cm−1 to 1475 cm−1 and from 843 cm−1 to 1190 cm−1 in the enlarged spectra (Fig. 9c, d), respectively. Two groups of peaks above represent CO of carboxy, CN of amine, and CO of phenyl-CHOH. It indicated that different reactions occurred between Co2+ and N-CQDs. Additionally, a peak from 1510 cm−1 to 1760 cm−1 could be seen from Fig. 9b. The peak represented CC of olefin increases rapidly and remained in equilibrium with the addition of Co2+. It is most likely due to the breaking of other bonds and the disappearance of the group on the surface of the N-CQDs leaving some of the groups encased inside exposed. Based on these, the fluorescence mechanism of N-CQDs for Co2+ was also proposed as Fig. 10 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cobalt ions types in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: There are two forms of cobalt ions in wastewater, C16H30O4Co, and CoSO4. In Fig. S5, stratification appeared, and a purple substance was generated when C8H16O2 was added to the wastewater. After FT-IR characterization, the purple substance was C16H30O4Co (Fig. S6).

According to the actual process flow in Fig. S7, it can be inferred that the wastewater contains CoSO4. Besides, the effect of C16H30O4Co on the fluorescence performance of N-CQDs is investigated in the concentration range of 0.001 to 30 mm (30 mM is close to the saturation concentration of C16H30O4Co in water). From Fig. S8(a), the fluorescent quenching degree (F/F0) showed a trend of increasing at low concentration and decreasing at high concentration. Under 302 nm UV light, the blue fluorescence dark a little, and orange precipitation was generated with increasing C16H30O4Co concentration. The F/F0 and C16H30O4Co concentration can be fitted nonlinearly (Fig. S8(b)). The equation is F/F0 = 0.8654 + 0.55/(1 + (x/0.0338)^0.79643), with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9829. It is worth noting that the maximum quenching rate of N-CQDs by C16H30O4Co was only 15.2%.

To exclude the influence of Na2SO4 on N-CQDs, real water samples were treated as seen in Fig. S9. Barium chloride (BaCl2) was added to the real water samples to generate a white precipitate, which XRD verified as Barium sulfate (BaSO4) (Fig. S10). Notably, there was no precipitated phase of Na2SO4 after the mixture of wastewater and N-CQDs.

Further, we detected the fluorescence spectra quickly after the mixed of preprocessed wastewater and N-CQDs. The detection results are shown in Fig. S8(c), which showed that the fluorescence quenching rate of N-CQDs was 55.6%. To sum up, Co2+ in the wastewater mainly existed in the form of CoSO4. Besides, it is worth noting that the fluorescence intensity of wastewater was close to zero under the same excitation wavelength and test conditions. Therefore, the fluorescence of the environmental background did not interfere with the application of N-CQDs, and the experimental results were reliable in this work.

In Fig. S11, soluble Co2+ in wastewater was transformed into insoluble orange solid after treatment of N-CQDs. ICP test shows that the concentration of Co2+ in wastewater is 1.73 × 105 ppb, the converted Co2+ concentration in solid is 33.333 mg/g. The concentration of Co2+ in the orange solid is 36.621 mg/g, which is within the allowable error range. The Co2+ concentration of the supernatant was 22 ppb after treatment, the calculated removal rate of Co2+ was 99.98%. Considering the above research results, the prepared N-CQDs were compared with the reported literature, and the results were shown in Table 1 (Kang et al., 2016; Kong et al., 2016; Kumar and Shim, 2009; Wang et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2010; Zhao et al., 2021). In conclusion, the prepared N-CQDs were entirely suitable for the specific detection and treatment of Co2+ in actual cobalt-containing wastewater.

This work synthesized novel nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) based on 1H-Benzotriazole (BTA) and choline chloride via a solvothermal method. The structural characterization revealed that the N-CQDs were spherical, distributed evenly, with an average size of 4.67 nm. Spectral investigation showed that the N-CQDs had good stability and anti-interference with strong resistance to highly saline conditions. The FLQY of was 25%. Spectroscopic studies and reactions showed that N-CQDs can be used to detect and treat Co2+ in wastewater. N-CQDs had large chromophoric groups (amide group, carboxyl group, formyl group) and auxochrome groups (oxhydryl, CO) on the surface causes N-CQDs to have a prominent emission peak at the excitation wavelength of 325 nm. It emitted bright blue fluorescence. The reaction of the chromophoric groups and Co2+ resulted in different degrees of fluorescence quenching after N-CQDs were mixed with Co2+.

Furthermore, the response of the N-CQDs to Co2+ under saline condition was linear in the range of 5 to 250 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2269 μM which was below emission standard 16.9684 μM. After eliminating the interference of other substances, the prepared N-CQDs were successfully tested on real wastewater with high salinity. A Co2+ removal rate of 99.98% was observed. Therefore, both detection and treatment were simultaneously achieved. N-CQDs' applicability for trace Co2+ detection and treatment in real water samples was demonstrated, which broadens its application in serving as potential nanoprobes in the future.
